intro: Almost every peach and nectarine sold is a named variety budded onto a rootstock, and the rootstock decides how the tree copes with your soil, your pests and your water. Most Australian peach rootstocks are peach seedlings chosen for vigour and root-knot nematode resistance, with a peach-almond hybrid for limey ground and a few size-controlling hybrids where you want a smaller tree. Peaches are also the one stone fruit that comes fairly true from seed, so a peach stone is the classic home-raised rootstock.
sources_note: Rootstock guidance on this page draws on horticultural research, university extension, the FAO review of Australian deciduous fruit production, and Australian and international nursery rootstock references.

sources.name: Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery: rootstocks for grafting
FAO: deciduous fruit production in Australia (regional rootstock tables)
Dave Wilson Nursery: grower rootstock comparisons
Four Winds Growers: deciduous rootstock information
University of Florida IFAS: rootstocks for Florida stone fruit (HS366)
Frontiers in Plant Science: nitrogen use by peach rootstock GF677 in alkaline soils
Olea Nurseries (Manjimup, WA): commercial rootstock range
Iowa State University Extension: germination of tree seed (stratification)
Philadelphia Orchard Project: growing peaches from seed

rootstocks.name: Nemaguard
rootstocks.soils: Prefers well-drained, sandy soils; poor on wet ground
rootstocks.notes: The standard peach and nectarine rootstock for lighter soils. Resistant to root-knot nematode, but susceptible to root-lesion nematode and to bacterial canker.

rootstocks.name: Lovell
rootstocks.soils: More tolerant of wet soils than Nemaguard, and more cold hardy
rootstocks.notes: The choice for heavier or colder ground, but susceptible to root-knot nematode on sandy soils, so it is the opposite trade-off to Nemaguard.

rootstocks.name: Golden Queen / Elberta
rootstocks.notes: Peach seedling rootstocks long used in Australia's temperate stone-fruit districts, alongside Nemaguard.

rootstocks.name: Okinawa, Flordaguard, Coastal
rootstocks.soils: Warm, low-chill and subtropical districts (Flordaguard not for alkaline soils)
rootstocks.notes: The low-chill peach rootstocks used for subtropical stone fruit in Australia. Coastal is a hardy peach that grows wild on the NSW north coast.

rootstocks.name: GF677
rootstocks.soils: Alkaline and calcareous soils; drought tolerant
rootstocks.notes: The rootstock for limey ground: it strongly resists lime-induced iron chlorosis where a peach seedling would go yellow. Sold in WA (wholesale) for peach and almond.

rootstocks.name: Citation
rootstocks.notes: A size-controlling hybrid where you want a shorter peach; resists root-knot nematode and copes with wet feet, but is incompatible with virus-carrying peach or nectarine scions.

grow_your_own.intro: Peaches come fairly true from seed, so a stone from a good peach makes a serviceable rootstock, which is why home grafters so often raise their own. The seedling will not be identical to its parent, but for a rootstock that does not matter; you bud your chosen variety onto it.

sections.heading: Raising a seedling from a stone
sections.body: Clean the flesh off the stone, then give the seed a cold, moist stratification of roughly three months (about 98 to 105 days) at around 1 to 5 degrees, for example in damp sand or a sand and peat mix in the fridge. Sow the stratified seed about 5cm (2 inches) deep in spring once the cold period is done.

sections.heading: From seedling to grafted tree
sections.body: A vigorous peach seedling is usually ready to bud or graft within a season or two, once the stem reaches roughly pencil thickness. Bud in late summer or graft your named variety in late winter. Commercial growers never fruit the seedling itself, because seedlings are too variable to sell as a known variety, but that same seedling is exactly what they bud a named variety onto.

faqs.q: Can I grow peach rootstock from a supermarket peach stone?
faqs.a: Yes. Peaches come fairly true from seed, so a stone from a good peach makes a usable rootstock. Give it about three months of cold, moist stratification in the fridge, sow it in spring, and bud or graft your chosen variety onto the seedling once it reaches pencil thickness. A grafted, named tree still fruits sooner and to type, so seed-raising is for the hobby grafter, not a shortcut to fruit.

faqs.q: Which peach rootstock suits sandy Perth soil?
faqs.a: A root-knot-nematode-resistant peach seedling such as Nemaguard is the classic choice for sandy soils, because root-knot nematode is the main sandy-soil problem for peaches. It prefers free-draining ground, so keep the water up in summer. On limey or alkaline sand, the peach-almond hybrid GF677 avoids the iron chlorosis that yellows a peach seedling.
